macro_architecture.reasoning: Map-Reduce analysis of 4 containers with 737 total components

layer_structure: Presentation Layer (Components, Directives, Pipes, Guards, Interceptors)
Infrastructure/Adapter Layer (Adapters, HTTP Interceptors, API Modules)

container_analyses.summary: The backend follows a classic layered architecture built on Spring Boot, exposing a REST API that orchestrates deed‑registry and document‑lifecycle management. While the technology stack (Spring MVC, Data JPA, Security, Hibernate, Scheduler) is solid, the implementation shows moderate separation of concerns, coupling, and cohesion. A large number of controllers, services, and entities create technical debt, and business logic is scattered across service classes without clear domain abstractions. Functional coverage is comprehensive, offering CRUD for deeds, handover datasets, signatures, archiving, key management, scheduled jobs, and reporting, but the codebase would benefit from stronger modularisation, DTO usage, and an application‑service façade.

integrations: Relational Database via Spring Data JPA / Hibernate
External Key Management Service (e.g., Xnp KM)
REST clients / external APIs via ProxyRestTemplate

functional.bounded_context: Deed Registry and Document Lifecycle Management
capabilities: Create, retrieve, update, lock and log deed entries
Manage handover data sets and their lifecycle
Store and retrieve document metadata
Handle signatures and participant information
Execute actions and scheduled jobs
Perform archiving operations and token signing
Manage cryptographic keys and re‑encryption processes
Process correction notes and business purposes
Generate and expose reports via REST
Provide health‑check and monitoring services

debt_indicators: Large controllers (32 REST controllers, many with >200 lines)
Service classes directly accessing repositories (tight coupling)
Entity explosion (199 entities) without clear bounded sub‑domains
Missing domain service abstractions – business logic scattered across services
Potential God services (42 service implementations, some very large)
Insufficient segregation of DTOs vs. entities in REST layer
recommendations: Refactor oversized controllers into smaller, purpose‑focused controllers or aggregate them by bounded context
Introduce clear domain service interfaces to decouple business logic from repository access
Group related entities into sub‑modules or packages reflecting bounded contexts to improve cohesion
Apply DTOs / mappers to separate API contracts from persistence models
Add façade or Application Service layer to orchestrate use‑cases, reducing direct controller‑to‑repository calls
Implement automated tests (unit & integration) for critical services to mitigate technical debt

container_analyses.overall_assessment: The backend provides a rich functional set and leverages a mature Spring ecosystem, but architectural degradation—large monolithic controllers/services, lack of domain‑service boundaries, and excessive entity count—drives a moderate C grade and hampers maintainability and scalability.
top_recommendations: Introduce a dedicated Application Service (façade) layer to orchestrate use‑cases and isolate controllers from repository logic.
Refactor oversized controllers and services, applying DTOs and mappers to enforce API‑persistence separation.
Re‑organize entities into bounded‑context sub‑modules and create domain service interfaces to improve cohesion and reduce coupling.

container_analyses.summary: The frontend container implements a component‑based Angular architecture organized into presentation, business‑logic, and infrastructure layers and communicates with the backend via REST APIs. It delivers a comprehensive set of legal‑deed management capabilities, from creation and workflow handling to reporting and user settings. Quality metrics indicate moderate separation of concerns, coupling, and cohesion, resulting in a grade of C. The primary technical debt stems from an excessive number of services, adapters, and pipes, and a lack of documented module relationships, which together threaten maintainability and scalability.

layers: Presentation Layer (Components, Directives, Pipes, Guards, Interceptors)
Infrastructure/Adapter Layer (Adapters, HTTP Interceptors, API Modules)

capabilities: Create, view, edit, approve and delete deeds
Manage deed workflows (finalize, validate signatures, reencryption)
Import and export document data (JSON, PDF, etc.)
Archive and retrieve documents
Handle user authentication and authorization
Configure and persist application settings
Display online help and notifications
Generate reports and metadata for deeds
Manage tasks, jobs and number assignments
Provide UI components for data entry, validation and correction notes
functional.bounded_context: Legal Deed Management and Workflow Processing

debt_indicators: Excessive number of services (131) suggesting possible God services
High count of adapters (50) indicating potential abstraction leakage
Large number of pipes (67) may lead to duplication and maintenance overhead
Lack of recorded component/module relations makes dependency analysis difficult
recommendations: Consolidate related services into feature‑scoped modules or facade services to reduce surface area
Introduce a clear adapter/factory abstraction layer to limit direct adapter usage across the app
Review and merge similar pipes; consider utility functions or shared pipe libraries
Document module boundaries and enforce dependency rules (e.g., via lint rules or architecture tests)
Limit the proliferation of adapters by defining generic adapter interfaces where appropriate
Implement architectural guardrails (e.g., layer enforcement, bounded context segregation) to improve separation of concerns

container_analyses.overall_assessment: The frontend delivers rich functional coverage and follows a sensible Angular component model, but its architectural health is hampered by service, adapter, and pipe bloat as well as undocumented module dependencies. Addressing these quality issues is essential to sustain future development velocity and system stability.
top_recommendations: Consolidate related services into feature‑scoped modules or facade services
Introduce a clear adapter/factory abstraction layer to curb adapter proliferation
Document module boundaries and enforce dependency rules via lint or architecture tests

layers: Presentation Layer (Components, Directives, Pipes, Guards, Interceptors)
Infrastructure/Adapter Layer (Adapters, HTTP Interceptors, API Modules)

executive_summary: Map-Reduce analysis completed for 4 containers with 737 components.
top_recommendations: Consider extracting large containers into separate services
Add API documentation for external interfaces
Implement health checks for all containers
